name: vit

members.name: ViTConfig
members.signature: ViTConfig(image_size: int = 224, patch_size: int = 16, num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, dim: int = 768, depth: int = 12, num_heads: int = 12, mlp_ratio: float = 4.0, dropout: float = 0.0, attention_dropout: float = 0.0)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(image_size: int = 224, patch_size: int = 16, num_classes: int = 1000, in_channels: int = 3, dim: int = 768, depth: int = 12, num_heads: int = 12, mlp_ratio: float = 4.0, dropout: float = 0.0, attention_dropout: float = 0.0)

members.summary: Configuration dataclass for every Vision Transformer (ViT) variant.
members.extended:
`ViTConfig` is an immutable container that fully specifies the
architecture of a Vision Transformer.  It is consumed by both
`ViT` (backbone) and `ViTForImageClassification`
(classifier head on top of the CLS token).  All canonical variants
described in [Dosovitskiy et al. (2020)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929) — Base / Large / Huge at patch
sizes 14, 16, and 32 — can be expressed by choosing different values for
`dim`, `depth`, `num_heads`, and `patch_size`.

The number of input tokens fed into the transformer encoder is
$N + 1$, where $N = (\text{image\_size} / \text{patch\_size})^2$
is the number of non-overlapping patches and the extra token is a
learnable `[CLS]` embedding prepended to the sequence.

parameters.name: image_size
parameters.description:
Spatial side length (in pixels) of the square input image.
`image_size` must be divisible by `patch_size`.  Defaults to
`224`, matching the ImageNet recipe in the original paper.

parameters.name: patch_size
parameters.description:
Side length of the non-overlapping square patches extracted by the
patch-embedding convolution.  Common values are `16`, `32`
(Base / Large) or `14` (Huge).  Defaults to `16`.

parameters.name: num_classes
parameters.description:
Number of output logits produced by the classification head.
Only used by `ViTForImageClassification`.  Defaults to
`1000` (ImageNet-1k).

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of input image channels.  Defaults to `3` (RGB).

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Hidden width $D$ of every transformer block — also the
dimensionality of the patch embedding and of the CLS token.
Defaults to `768` (ViT-Base).

parameters.name: depth
parameters.description:
Number of stacked transformer encoder blocks $L$.
Defaults to `12` (ViT-Base).

parameters.name: num_heads
parameters.description:
Number of attention heads inside each multi-head self-attention
block.  `dim` must be divisible by `num_heads`.  Defaults to
`12`.

parameters.name: mlp_ratio
parameters.description:
Expansion ratio of the MLP block.  The hidden width of the MLP is
`int(dim * mlp_ratio)`; the standard recipe is `4.0` (i.e. a
4x expansion).  Defaults to `4.0`.

parameters.name: dropout
parameters.description:
Dropout probability applied (i) after the patch embedding +
positional embedding sum and (ii) inside the MLP feed-forward
sub-layer.  Defaults to `0.0`.

parameters.name: attention_dropout
parameters.description:
Dropout probability applied to the post-softmax attention weights
inside every multi-head self-attention block.  Defaults to `0.0`.

examples:
Build a custom ViT-Base/16 configuration for CIFAR-10 (10 classes,
32x32 inputs, patch size 4 — yields 64 tokens):
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import ViTConfig
>>> cfg = ViTConfig(
...     image_size=32,
...     patch_size=4,
...     num_classes=10,
...     dim=768,
...     depth=12,
...     num_heads=12,
... )
>>> cfg.image_size, cfg.patch_size, cfg.num_classes
(32, 4, 10)
Reuse a canonical variant and override only the number of classes:
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit._pretrained import _CFG_B16
>>> from dataclasses import replace
>>> cfg = replace(_CFG_B16, num_classes=100)
>>> cfg.dim, cfg.depth, cfg.num_classes
(768, 12, 100)
notes:
The canonical variants registered as factory functions in
`lucid.models.vision.vit` are (see Table 1 of the paper):

============== ===== ====== ======= ===========
Variant        dim   depth  heads   patch sizes
============== ===== ====== ======= ===========
ViT-Base       768   12     12      16, 32
ViT-Large      1024  24     16      16, 32
ViT-Huge       1280  32     16      14
============== ===== ====== ======= ===========

Reference: Alexey Dosovitskiy *et al.*, *"An Image is Worth 16x16
Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale"*, ICLR 2021,
`[arXiv:2010.11929](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929) <https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929>`_.

attributes.name: model_type
attributes.description:
Constant string `"vit"` used by the model registry to associate
config instances with the ViT model class.

members.name: ViT

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the Flatten module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Update src CLS token using cross-attention over tgt tokens.

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: (B, N_src, dim_src) — source branch tokens (CLS at index 0)

parameters.name: tgt
parameters.description: (B, N_tgt, dim_tgt) — target branch tokens

members.extended:
A ViT first splits the input image into a sequence of non-overlapping
patches and linearly projects each patch into a $D$-dimensional
embedding.  A learnable `[CLS]` token is prepended, a learnable
positional embedding is added, and the resulting sequence is processed
by `depth` stacked pre-norm transformer encoder blocks.  The final
representation returned by `forward_features` is the CLS token
embedding after a final LayerNorm — the standard feature used for
classification or transfer learning.

Concretely, the input sequence fed to the encoder is

$$
z_0 = [\,x_{\text{class}};\, x_p^1 E;\, x_p^2 E;\, \dots;\,
          x_p^N E\,] + E_{\text{pos}},
$$
where $x_p^i \in \mathbb{R}^{P^2 C}$ is the $i$-th flattened
patch, $E \in \mathbb{R}^{P^2 C \times D}$ is the patch projection,
$E_{\text{pos}} \in \mathbb{R}^{(N+1) \times D}$ is the learnable
positional embedding, and $N = (\text{image\_size} / \text{patch\_size})^2$.

Use this backbone when you want features rather than logits — e.g.
fine-tuning on a custom downstream head, contrastive pretraining, or
feature extraction for retrieval.  For end-to-end image classification,
use `ViTForImageClassification` instead.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen dataclass specifying `image_size`, `patch_size`, `dim`,
`depth`, `num_heads`, `mlp_ratio`, `dropout`,
`attention_dropout`, and `in_channels`.  See `ViTConfig`.

examples:
Build a ViT-Base/16 backbone and run a single forward pass:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import ViT, ViTConfig
>>> cfg = ViTConfig()              # ViT-Base/16 defaults
>>> model = ViT(cfg)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> feat = model.forward_features(x)
>>> feat.shape                     # (B, dim)
(1, 768)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape    # (B, 1, dim)
(1, 1, 768)
notes:
The classification head is **not** included in this class — only the
backbone trunk.  `forward_features` returns a flat
$(B, D)$ feature tensor, whereas `forward` wraps it in a
`BaseModelOutput` whose `last_hidden_state` has shape
$(B, 1, D)$ for spatial consistency with other vision models.

Reference: Alexey Dosovitskiy *et al.*, *"An Image is Worth 16x16
Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale"*, ICLR 2021,
`[arXiv:2010.11929](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929) <https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929>`_.

attributes.name: patch_embed
attributes.description: Patch-extraction convolution producing the initial token sequence.

attributes.name: cls_token
attributes.description:
Learnable `[CLS]` token, shape `(1, 1, dim)`.  Initialized with
truncated normal $\mathcal{N}(0, 0.02^2)$.

attributes.name: pos_embed
attributes.description:
Learnable positional embedding, shape `(1, num_patches + 1, dim)`.
Initialized with truncated normal $\mathcal{N}(0, 0.02^2)$.

attributes.name: pos_drop
attributes.description: Dropout applied right after adding positional embeddings.

attributes.name: norm
attributes.description: Final LayerNorm applied before extracting the CLS token.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Single-stage feature description with channel count equal to
`config.dim` and spatial reduction equal to `config.patch_size`.

members.name: ViTForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)
methods.summary: Update src CLS token using cross-attention over tgt tokens.

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: (B, N_src, dim_src) — source branch tokens (CLS at index 0)

parameters.name: tgt
parameters.description: (B, N_tgt, dim_tgt) — target branch tokens

members.summary: Vision Transformer with a linear classification head on the CLS token.
members.extended:
Wraps the same trunk as `ViT` (patch embedding -> positional
embedding -> `depth` transformer blocks -> final LayerNorm) and adds
a single `nn.Linear` classification head that maps the final
CLS-token feature of dimension $D$ to `config.num_classes`
logits:

$$
\text{logits} = W_{\text{head}}\, z_L^{[0]} + b_{\text{head}},
\qquad W_{\text{head}} \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times D}
$$
where $z_L^{[0]}$ is the post-LayerNorm CLS token at the final
layer and $C$ is the number of classes.  This is the standard
ImageNet recipe described in the original ViT paper.

Pass `labels` to `forward` to compute the cross-entropy loss
in the same pass — handy for HuggingFace-style training loops.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture specification.  Must set `num_classes` to the
desired number of output categories (default 1000 for ImageNet-1k).
See `ViTConfig`.

examples:
End-to-end inference with the default ViT-Base/16 classifier:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import (
...     ViTConfig, ViTForImageClassification
... )
>>> cfg = ViTConfig(num_classes=1000)
>>> model = ViTForImageClassification(cfg)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
Pass labels for joint loss computation during training:
>>> labels = lucid.tensor([0])
>>> out = model(x, labels=labels)
>>> out.loss.shape
()
notes:
Reference: Alexey Dosovitskiy *et al.*, *"An Image is Worth 16x16
Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale"*, ICLR 2021,
`[arXiv:2010.11929](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929) <https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929>`_.

attributes.name: patch_embed
attributes.description: Patch-extraction convolution producing the initial token sequence.

attributes.name: pos_embed
attributes.description: Learnable positional embedding, shape `(1, num_patches + 1, dim)`.

attributes.name: pos_drop
attributes.description: Dropout applied right after adding positional embeddings.

attributes.name: norm
attributes.description: Final LayerNorm applied before extracting the CLS token.

attributes.name: head
attributes.description:
Classification head with shape `(num_classes, dim)`.  The
attribute is intentionally named `head` so pretrained
state-dicts using the standard `head.weight` / `head.bias` key
names load directly.

members.name: vit_base_16
members.extended:
Builds the canonical *ViT-Base* configuration with patch size 16:
`dim=768`, `depth=12`, `num_heads=12`, `mlp_ratio=4.0`.  With
the default 224x224 input this yields $(224/16)^2 = 196$ patch
tokens plus one CLS token, for an input sequence length of `197` to
the transformer encoder.  Approximately **86M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when available
in the model zoo.  Defaults to `False` (random initialization).

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides applied on top of the canonical ViT-Base/16
config — e.g. `image_size=384`, `in_channels=1`, or
`dropout=0.1`.  Each override must match a field of
`ViTConfig`.

returns.description:
A `ViT` backbone instance returning a flat `(B, 768)` CLS
feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_base_16
>>> model = vit_base_16()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> feat = model.forward_features(x)
>>> feat.shape
(1, 768)
notes:
ViT-Base/16 was pretrained on JFT-300M / ImageNet-21k and reported
**84.15% top-1 / 97.20% top-5 on ImageNet-1k** after fine-tuning at
384x384 in [Dosovitskiy et al. (2020, Table 5)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929).  See
`[arXiv:2010.11929](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929) <https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929>`_.

members.name: vit_base_16_cls
members.extended:
Combines the `vit_base_16` backbone with a single
`nn.Linear` classification head on the CLS token.  Default
output is `num_classes=1000` (ImageNet-1k); override via
`num_classes=...`.  Approximately **86M parameters** for the
1000-class head.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-21k pretrained weights for the trunk
(and fine-tuned head when available).  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Base/16 config.
Commonly used: `num_classes` to switch label space,
`image_size` to fine-tune at higher resolution.

returns.description:
Classifier returning `ImageClassificationOutput` whose
`logits` has shape `(B, num_classes)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_base_16_cls
>>> model = vit_base_16_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Standard ImageNet recipe: backbone returns the CLS token after the
final LayerNorm, head applies a single affine map to produce class
logits.  See `[arXiv:2010.11929](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929) <https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929>`_.

members.name: vit_base_32
members.extended:
Same ViT-Base trunk as `vit_base_16` (`dim=768`, `depth=12`,
`num_heads=12`) but with patch size 32, which reduces the sequence
length to $(224/32)^2 = 49$ patch tokens (+1 CLS = 50).  The
larger patches trade fine-grained spatial detail for ~4x cheaper
self-attention and ~88M parameters in total.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when available.
Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Base/32 config.

returns.description: A `ViT` backbone returning a flat `(B, 768)` CLS feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_base_32
>>> model = vit_base_32()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 768)
notes:
Reference: [Dosovitskiy et al. (2020)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929), Table 1.  The `/32` variant
is most useful when latency / FLOPs dominate over absolute accuracy.

members.name: vit_base_32_cls
members.extended:
Combines the `vit_base_32` backbone (`dim=768`, `depth=12`,
`num_heads=12`, patch size 32) with a linear classification head on
the CLS token.  The `/32` patching yields a 49-token sequence,
making this the cheapest ViT-Base classifier.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when available.
Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Base/32 config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_base_32_cls
>>> model = vit_base_32_cls(num_classes=10)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 10)

members.name: vit_large_16
members.extended:
The canonical *ViT-Large* configuration with patch size 16:
`dim=1024`, `depth=24`, `num_heads=16`, `mlp_ratio=4.0`.  With
a 224x224 input it produces 197 tokens (196 patches + CLS) feeding 24
pre-norm transformer blocks of width 1024.  Approximately
**307M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when available.
Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Large/16 config.

returns.description: A `ViT` backbone returning a flat `(B, 1024)` CLS feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_large_16
>>> model = vit_large_16()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 1024)
notes:
ViT-Large/16 reaches **85.30% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** when fine-tuned
at 384x384 after ImageNet-21k pretraining (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020,
Table 5).  Best accuracy/cost trade-off among the Large variants.

members.name: vit_large_16_cls
members.extended:
Combines the `vit_large_16` backbone (`dim=1024`,
`depth=24`, `num_heads=16`, patch size 16) with a linear
classification head on the CLS token.  Approximately
**307M parameters**.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when available.
Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Large/16 config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_large_16_cls
>>> model = vit_large_16_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Reaches **85.30% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 384x384 fine-tune
resolution after ImageNet-21k pretraining (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020,
Table 5).

members.name: vit_large_32
members.extended:
Same ViT-Large trunk as `vit_large_16` (`dim=1024`,
`depth=24`, `num_heads=16`) but with patch size 32, producing only
$(224/32)^2 = 49$ patch tokens (+1 CLS).  Approximately
**307M parameters** with substantially reduced attention cost.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when available.
Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Large/32 config.

returns.description: A `ViT` backbone returning a flat `(B, 1024)` CLS feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_large_32
>>> model = vit_large_32()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 1024)
notes:
Reference: [Dosovitskiy et al. (2020)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929), Table 1.  Useful when Large
capacity is desired but the `/16` token count is too expensive.

members.name: vit_large_32_cls
members.extended:
Combines the `vit_large_32` backbone (`dim=1024`,
`depth=24`, `num_heads=16`, patch size 32) with a linear
classification head on the CLS token.  The `/32` patching gives a
49-token sequence and substantially lowers self-attention cost.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when available.
Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Large/32 config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_large_32_cls
>>> model = vit_large_32_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)

members.name: vit_huge_14
members.extended:
The canonical *ViT-Huge* configuration with patch size 14:
`dim=1280`, `depth=32`, `num_heads=16`, `mlp_ratio=4.0`.  With
a 224x224 input it produces $(224/14)^2 = 256$ patch tokens
(+1 CLS = 257 total) feeding 32 transformer blocks of width 1280.
Approximately **632M parameters** — the largest variant in the
paper.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads JFT-300M / ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when
available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Huge/14 config.

returns.description: A `ViT` backbone returning a flat `(B, 1280)` CLS feature.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_huge_14
>>> model = vit_huge_14()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model.forward_features(x).shape
(1, 1280)
notes:
ViT-Huge/14 achieves **88.55% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** when fine-tuned
at 518x518 after JFT-300M pretraining (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020,
Table 2) — the state-of-the-art result reported in the paper at
release.  Training from scratch on ImageNet-1k alone is impractical
at this scale; pretrained weights are strongly recommended.

members.name: vit_huge_14_cls
members.extended:
Combines the `vit_huge_14` backbone (`dim=1280`, `depth=32`,
`num_heads=16`, patch size 14) with a linear classification head on
the CLS token.  Approximately **632M parameters** — the largest ViT
variant in the paper.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
If `True`, loads JFT-300M / ImageNet-21k pretrained weights when
available.  Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description: Keyword overrides on top of the canonical ViT-Huge/14 config.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.vit import vit_huge_14_cls
>>> model = vit_huge_14_cls(num_classes=1000)
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
>>> model(x).logits.shape
(1, 1000)
notes:
Achieves **88.55% top-1 on ImageNet-1k** at 518x518 fine-tune
resolution after JFT-300M pretraining (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020,
Table 2) — the headline result of the paper.

summary: Vision Transformer (ViT) model family — Dosovitskiy et al., 2020.
extended:
This sub-package exposes the canonical Vision Transformer architecture
introduced in *"An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image
Recognition at Scale"* (ICLR 2021, `[arXiv:2010.11929](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929)
<https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929>`_).

The architecture splits an image into non-overlapping square patches,
linearly projects each patch into a $D$-dimensional embedding,
prepends a learnable `[CLS]` token, adds a learnable positional
embedding, and processes the resulting sequence with a stack of pre-norm
transformer encoder blocks.  The final CLS-token representation is used
as the global image feature.
notes:
Configuration:

* `ViTConfig` — immutable dataclass describing any ViT variant.

Modules:

* `ViT` — feature-extractor backbone (returns `(B, dim)` CLS).
* `ViTForImageClassification` — backbone + linear head producing
  classification logits.

Backbone factories:

* `vit_base_16`, `vit_base_32`
* `vit_large_16`, `vit_large_32`
* `vit_huge_14`

Classifier factories (CLS-token linear head):

* `vit_base_16_cls`, `vit_base_32_cls`
* `vit_large_16_cls`, `vit_large_32_cls`
* `vit_huge_14_cls`
citation: Dosovitskiy, Alexey, et al. "An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale." International Conference on Learning Representations, 2021.
theory:
The Vision Transformer (ViT) shows that convolutions are *not*
required for state-of-the-art image classification given enough
data.  An input image
$x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ is reshaped into
a sequence of non-overlapping square patches
$x_p \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (P^2 C)}$ with
$N = HW / P^2$, each patch flattened and projected to a
$D$-dimensional token by a single linear map.  A learnable
class token $x_{\text{cls}}$ is prepended, and learnable
positional embeddings $E_{\text{pos}}$ are added:



$$
z_0 = [\,x_{\text{cls}};\; x_p^1 E;\; \dots;\; x_p^N E\,] + E_{\text{pos}}.
$$

The sequence then flows through $L$ stacked transformer
encoder blocks — multi-head self-attention plus an MLP, each
wrapped by LayerNorm and residual connections.  Classification
reads the final state of the class token through a single linear
head.

The paper's central empirical claim is that the inductive biases
convolutions provide (locality, translation equivariance) become
*unnecessary* once pre-training data is large enough — ViT
pre-trained on ImageNet-21k or JFT-300M matches or beats the best
ResNet/EfficientNet baselines on downstream benchmarks while
using fewer FLOPs.  Three canonical variants from Table 1 (Base /
Large / Huge) trade compute for accuracy by scaling
$(D, L, H)$ — the hidden width, depth, and number of
attention heads.
